risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "The stratified randomization was performed by a statistician at All India Institute of Medical Sciences (AIIMS, New Delhi) who was not involved in implementation of the intervention... The PHCs in each of the two strata were randomly allocated into an intervention and a control group. The randomization was done using the software nQuery." Comment: This describes a stratified random allocation carried out by an independent statistician using software, which is sufficient to indicate a proper random sequence generation method.

Quote: "The stratified randomization was performed by a statistician at All India Institute of Medical Sciences (AIIMS, New Delhi) who was not involved in implementation of the intervention... The PHCs in each of the two strata were randomly allocated into an intervention and a control group. The randomization was done using the software nQuery." Comment: Although sequence generation and an independent statistician are described, the report does not state how the allocation was concealed from those enrolling the PHCs or whether any mechanism (e.g., central allocation, SNOSE, or identical containers) prevented foreknowledge of the next assignment. Therefore allocation concealment is not explicitly demonstrated.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Due to the nature of the community-based intervention, blinding was not possible." Comment: Participants and the personnel delivering the intervention (ASHAs, PHC staff, mHealth facilitators, and counsellors) were not blinded. Because the intervention required active use of smartphones/web tools, training, mentoring, and incentives, knowledge of allocation could plausibly influence the care delivered and the service-coverage outcomes assessed in this trial.

Summary: The protocol states that "blinding was not possible" due to the community-based intervention, but it does not explicitly report whether outcome assessors were blinded. Outcomes were measured mainly by household surveys "as reported by Type-A respondents (mothers...)" and "Type-B respondents (mothers...)" and by a data collection team, with a separate team handling surveillance; because the main outcomes were largely patient self-report and the blinding status of participants/outcome assessors is not clearly stated, the risk of detection bias cannot be judged confidently. Comment: Since the patient is effectively the assessor for self-reported outcomes, and participant blinding is not described, this remains unclear rather than low risk.

Quote: "all eligible women that were not absent on the day of the surveys were included." Comment: The study used baseline and endline household surveys with ongoing pregnancy/mortality surveillance, and the available description suggests essentially complete inclusion of eligible respondents present at survey time. No differential dropout, exclusions after randomization, or substantial missing outcome data are reported, so attrition is unlikely to have biased the results.
